10.1016/j.jmp.2021.102621
Competence-based extension of knowledge structure theory (CbKST;  Doignon, 1994a; Düntsch & Gediga, 1995; Falmagne, Koppen, Villano, Doignon, & Johanessen, 1990; Gediga & Düntsch, 2002; Heller, Ünlü, and Albert, 2013; Korossy, 1997, 1999 ) provides the theoretical framework for assessing the set of skills an individual has available in a certain disciplinary domain (which is called the  competence state ) from the responses of this individual to test items. The term “skills” is used here to refer to the latent variables under investigation. This term fits particularly well in an educational assessment, whereas it may be less appropriate in other areas where CbKST applies. For instance, in clinical diagnostic tests the latent variables of interest might be better referred to as the diagnostic criteria that are related to a particular mental disorder. In the different editions of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM), most mental disorders are defined using a set of diagnostic criteria that may be met or not. A multitude of tests was developed to measure specific disorders based on the DSM criteria. The criteria that an individual meets are assessed from the responses to the items of these tests.

In CbKST, the relationship between the available skills and the solution behavior is characterized by two mappings, known as the  skill function  and the  problem function  ( Düntsch & Gediga, 1995 ). The skill function assigns to each item one or more subsets of skills, each of which is sufficient for solving the item. The problem function assigns to each set of skills the set of items that, according to the skill function, can be solved with those skills. This set of items is the  knowledge state  delineated by the set of skills, and it represents the solution behavior that is expected under ideal conditions (i.e., in the absence of careless errors and lucky guesses) for a person having available the respective set of skills.

In order to infer the latent competence state of an individual from the observed responses to test items, two separate tasks need to be accomplished. First, the latent knowledge state must be inferred from the responses to the items. This process is usually based on a probabilistic framework ( Falmagne & Doignon, 1988, 1988b ) defined on a collection of knowledge states called  knowledge structure . Second, the competence state must be derived from the knowledge state. This process is deterministic, and it is based on the problem function. Sometimes there is a unique competence state underlying the knowledge state, other times the same knowledge state is delineated by more than one competence state. A good test ensures that the uncertainty about the competence state underlying the knowledge state is as small as possible.

Recently,  Heller, Anselmi, Stefanutti, and Robusto (2017) ,  Heller, Stefanutti, Anselmi, and Robusto (2015, 2016)  have identified properties of tests that allow for singling out a unique competence state from the knowledge state. In the work of these authors, a test already exists and the goal is to understand whether this test allows for unique assessment or not. However, several questions are still unanswered. Given that the test does not allow unique assessment, how can it be improved so that unique assessment is obtained? Is it possible to shorten a test without impairing its property to identify the competence state? What to do if the test does not exist yet and it has to be constructed from scratch?

The present work strives to answer all the aforementioned questions.  Competence-based test development  (CbTD) is introduced as a novel approach for the development of tests for skill assessment. 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         The present work formalizes and integrates contributions presented at various conferences ( Anselmi, 2019; Anselmi, Heller, Stefanutti, & Robusto, 2018, 2019 ). 
                       Given a fixed collection of skills that are of interest in a certain disciplinary domain, tests are constructed that are as informative as possible about individuals’ competence states. The proposed methodology allows for developing two common types of tests, the so-called  conjunctive  and  disjunctive  tests. In conjunctive tests, all skills associated with an item are necessary for solving it. In disjunctive tests, having available any of the skills associated with an item is sufficient for solving it. Conjunctive tests are particularly suitable in educational assessments, where the solution of an item is usually broken into a series of steps, all of which must be successfully performed to provide a correct response to the item. Disjunctive tests are particularly suitable in medical and psychological assessments, where diagnoses are typically made based on the presence of only some of the possible symptoms ( Roussos, Templin, & Henson, 2007; Templin & Henson, 2006 ). Potential applications of CbTD cover different scenarios, such as the construction of a test from scratch, and the improvement or shortening of an existing test.

The theoretical framework behind CbTD is CbKST, an extension of knowledge structure theory (KST;  Doignon & Falmagne, 1985, 1999 ) that takes into account the skills underlying the knowledge states. Applications of CbKST can be found, for instance, in  Albert and Lukas (1999), Anselmi, Robusto, and Stefanutti (2012, 2013), de Chiusole, Stefanutti, Anselmi, and Robusto (2020), Heller, Augustin, Hockemeyer, Stefanutti, and Albert (2013), Heller, Steiner, Hockemeyer, and Albert (2006), Robusto, Stefanutti, and Anselmi (2010)  and  Stefanutti, Anselmi, and Robusto (2011) . From CbKST, CbTD inherits its basic concepts and the mathematical notation based on set theory. Other concepts and ideas have been inspired by rough set theory (RST;  Pawlak, 1982, 1991 ), a formal theory with a broad spectrum of applications in data analysis, data mining, and machine learning. In particular, the work conducted by  Yao and Zhao (2009)  on information systems has been a source of inspiration.

It is worth noting that CbKST is not the only theoretical framework that allows skill assessment based on item responses. There is at least another approach based on cognitive diagnostic models (CDMs;  DiBello, Roussos, & Stout, 2007; DiBello & Stout, 2007; Rupp, Templin, & Henson, 2010; Tatsuoka, 1990 ). While CbKST and CDMs developed in parallel for decades,  Heller et al. (2015, 2016)  pointed out that there is a close correspondence between these two frameworks which ranges from very basic concepts to specific models. The present work is expected to be of interest also to practitioners in CDMs.

The article is organized as follows. Section  2  outlines basic concepts of CbKST that are useful to the subsequent developments in CbTD. Section  3  proposes a characterization of tests that is based on their informativeness about the competence states underlying the knowledge states. Section  4  outlines canonical procedures for developing tests that are based on recent results in CbKST, and illustrates their application for constructing a test from scratch, improving and shortening an existing test. Sections  5 and 6  introduce basic concepts that are at the foundation of a more general procedure for test development that is described in Section  7 . Section  8  presents applications of this procedure for constructing a test from scratch, improving and shortening an existing test. Section  9  explores connections between CbTD and RST. In Sections  4  to  9 , the focus is on the development of conjunctive tests. Section  10  extends the methods described in the previous sections in several directions, including the development of disjunctive tests, and outlines some paths for future research.

                       of dichotomous items, the knowledge state of a person is the collection

                         Q

                            C

                            S 
                         
                       of all the skills that this person has available. A competence structure is a pair

                       and

                      , respectively. In what follows, the collection  
                         Q 
                       will be sometimes called a  test .

Other conditions, denoted as  witness condition  and  generalized witness condition  ( Heller et al., 2017, 2015, 2016 ) are sufficient but not necessary for the problem function induced by a conjunctive competence model to be injective. Identifiability, witness, and generalized witness conditions are meant to evaluate the injectiveness of problem functions induced by conjunctive competence models. However, thanks to the duality between conjunctive and disjunctive models, these three conditions can also be used to evaluate the injectiveness of problems functions corresponding to disjunctive competence models.                                         .

                                        ]

                                        C 
                                        ] 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           p

                             be the equivalence classes induced by  
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     1

                            , respectively. Competence model

                            for at least one

                                  ∈

                                     M

                             is said to be  weakly more informative  than competence model  
                               
                                  
                                     M

                            , denoted as  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        M 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ≿

                            for all  
                               
                                  C 
                                  ∈ 
                                  C 
                               
                            . Competence model  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             is said to be  equally informative  as competence model  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            , denoted as  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        M 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∼ 
                                  
                                     
                                        M 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            , if  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              M 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        ≿ 
                                        
                                           
                                              M 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                         and  
                                        
                                           
                                              M 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                        ≿ 
                                        
                                           
                                              M 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        .

Intuitively speaking, more informative means that the ability of a competence model to uncover a competence state from the knowledge state is larger than that of another competence model, whereas weakly more informative means that the ability of that model is no less. Equally informative means that two competence models have the same ability of uncovering the competence states from the knowledge states.

Let  
                         M 
                       be the set of every possible competence model  
                         
                            M 
                            = 
                            
                               ( 
                               Q 
                               , 
                               S 
                               , 
                               C 
                               , 
                               μ 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       defined on the fixed collections  
                         S 
                       and  
                         C 
                      , where  
                         Q 
                       is any arbitrary finite set. A relation  
                         ≿ 
                       is defined on  
                         M 
                       according to  Definition 1 , which is a quasi-order (reflexive and transitive relation). Its symmetric part  
                         ∼ 
                       is an equivalence relation (reflexive, symmetric, and transitive relation) and its asymmetric part  
                         ≻ 
                       is a strict partial order (asymmetric and transitive relation). Since the relation  
                         ≿ 
                       defined on  
                         M 
                       is a quasi-order, in general there are competence models in  
                         M 
                       that are incomparable with respect to informativeness. Moreover, we note in passing that the quotient set of  
                         M 
                       is isomorphic to the lattice of all the partitions of  
                         C 
                      .

In particular applications, there may be cases in which only some competence models in  
                         M 
                       can be built. There may be empirical settings where an item requiring a specific competency  
                         
                            T 
                            ⊆ 
                            S 
                         
                       to be solved might be difficult to find or simply does not exist. In these cases, it is useful to confine consideration to those competence models in  
                         M 
                       that can be built, whose collection is denoted with  
                         
                            N 
                            ⊆ 
                            M 
                         
                      .

Definition 2 
                         Maximally Informative and Fully Informative Competence Models 
                         A competence model  
                               
                                  M 
                                  ∈ 
                                  N 
                               
                             is said to be  maximally informative  in  
                               N 
                             if there is no competence model  
                               
                                  N 
                                  ∈ 
                                  N 
                               
                             that is more informative than  
                               M 
                            . A competence model  
                               
                                  M 
                                  ∈ 
                                  M 
                               
                             is said to be  fully informative  if it is maximally informative in  
                               M 
                            .

A competence model  
                               
                                  M 
                                  ∈ 
                                  N 
                               
                             is said to be  maximally informative  in  
                               N 
                             if there is no competence model  
                               
                                  N 
                                  ∈ 
                                  N 
                               
                             that is more informative than  
                               M 
                            . A competence model  
                               
                                  M 
                                  ∈ 
                                  M 
                               
                             is said to be  fully informative  if it is maximally informative in  
                               M 
                            .

Remark 1 
                         In a fully informative competence model, every equivalence class  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           [ 
                                           C 
                                           ] 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  C 
                                  / 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             is a singleton (which in turn is equivalent to stating that the delineated problem function  
                               p 
                             is injective).

In a fully informative competence model, every equivalence class  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           [ 
                                           C 
                                           ] 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  C 
                                  / 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             is a singleton (which in turn is equivalent to stating that the delineated problem function  
                               p 
                             is injective).

Definition 3 
                         Irreducible Competence Model 
                         A competence model  
                               
                                  M 
                                  ∈ 
                                  N 
                               
                             is said to be  irreducible  in  
                               N 
                             if it is more informative than any proper domain restriction of it in  
                               N 
                            .

A competence model  
                               
                                  M 
                                  ∈ 
                                  N 
                               
                             is said to be  irreducible  in  
                               N 
                             if it is more informative than any proper domain restriction of it in  
                               N 
                            .

Maximal informativeness ensures that the ability of uncovering the competence states from the knowledge states is maximum given the competence models that can be built (i.e., that are included in  
                         
                            N 
                            ⊆ 
                            M 
                         
                      ). Full informativeness ensures that, whatever the competence state of the person, it can be uniquely uncovered from the knowledge state. Irreducibility means that each of the items in  
                         Q 
                       is necessary to distinguish any two competence states from the knowledge states.

Some simple facts follow. A fully informative competence model is weakly more informative than any other competence model defined on the same collections  
                         S 
                       and  
                         C 
                      . This is because all equivalence classes induced by a fully informative competence model are singletons and, as such, they are subsets of the corresponding equivalence classes induced by any other competence model. Moreover, if two competence models defined on the same collections  
                         S 
                       and  
                         C 
                       are fully informative, then they are also equally informative. This is because two fully informative competence models induce equivalence classes that are all singletons, thus resulting in the same quotient set of equivalence classes.

The following example illustrates the competence models that have been described in this section. For competence states and competencies, the shorthand notation  
                         
                            x 
                            y 
                         
                       is used to denote the set  
                         
                            { 
                            x 
                            , 
                            y 
                            } 
                         
                      .

Competence models  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     5 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     6 
                                  
                               
                             are maximally informative in  
                               N 
                            , whereas  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     4 
                                  
                               
                             are not. Furthermore,  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     5 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     6 
                                  
                               
                             are fully informative and more informative than  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     4 
                                  
                               
                            . Competence models  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     4 
                                  
                               
                             are equally informative, whereas they are incomparable to  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                             with respect to informativeness. Competence models  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     4 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     5 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     6 
                                  
                               
                             are irreducible.

Let us consider competence model

Competence models  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                             are equally informative as their respective proper domain restrictions  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                            .

Any two items  
                         
                            q 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                       are said to be  redundant  if they are associated with exactly the same competencies (i.e., if  
                         
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      ) and, according to  Doignon and Falmagne (1999) , they are said to be  equally informative  if they belong to exactly the same knowledge states (i.e., if  
                         
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            p 
                            
                               ( 
                               C 
                               ) 
                            
                            ⟺ 
                            
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            p 
                            
                               ( 
                               C 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       for every  
                         
                            C 
                            ∈ 
                            C 
                         
                      ).

Lemma 1  immediately follows from the definition of the problem function in  (1) .

Lemma 1 
                         
                            If two items  
                            
                               
                                  q 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                             
                            are redundant, then they are also equally informative.

If two items  
                            
                               
                                  q 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                             
                            are redundant, then they are also equally informative.

The following example shows that the converse implication does not hold.

Example 2 
                         Let us consider the conjunctive competence model  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  C 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             where  
                               
                                  Q 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     1 
                                     , 
                                     2 
                                     , 
                                     3 
                                     , 
                                     4 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  S 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     c 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  C 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     ∅ 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     c 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     c 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     b 
                                     c 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            , and

Lemma 2 
                         
                            Any irreducible competence model

It is worth noting that the absence of redundant items in a competence model does not imply that this model is irreducible. For instance, competence models  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               3 
                            
                         
                       in  Example 1  do not contain redundant items, but are not irreducible.

In what follows, tests are called “more informative”, “fully informative”, “irreducible” and so on, whenever these properties hold for the respective competence models.

This section outlines specific procedures for developing fully informative tests for a given competence structure  
                         C 
                       which might be called canonical, as they exploit properties of  
                         C 
                       in a quite natural way. The procedures are based on the notion of atoms and draw upon the witness and generalized witness conditions (see Section  2 ), which proved useful in characterizing unique skill assessment ( Heller et al., 2017, 2015, 2016 ).

Building upon these notions, consider a conjunctive competence model  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            C 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                       satisfying the witness condition and the property  
                         
                            (3) 
                            
                               
                                  μ 
                                   is a bijection from  
                                  Q 
                                   onto  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                               
                            
                         
                      with  
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               C 
                            
                         
                       denoting the set of atoms of  
                         C 
                      . Then, by construction, the competence model does not contain redundant items, is fully informative, and unique up to permutation of items, which amounts to consider different bijections in  (3) . However, it need not be irreducible. In order to assure this property, let  
                         C 
                       be such that  
                         
                            (4) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     for every atom  
                                     A 
                                     ∈ 
                                     
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                        
                                           C 
                                        
                                     
                                      there is a competence state  
                                     C 
                                  
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     = 
                                     ⋃ 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        U 
                                        ∈ 
                                        C 
                                        : 
                                        U 
                                        ⊂ 
                                        A 
                                        } 
                                     
                                     ∈ 
                                     C 
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      We note in passing that every competence space (i.e, union-closed competence structure) satisfies  (4) , which in turn is sufficient for the competence model to be irreducible, as the following result shows.

Proposition 1 
                         
                            Let

                                  ,

                                  ,

Notice that the converse of  Proposition 1  need not hold in general.

The following discusses the application of canonical procedures to the different scenarios of constructing a test from scratch, and of improving and shortening an existing test.

Given an arbitrary competence structure  
                            C 
                          on  
                            S 
                         , we can construct a conjunctive competence model  
                            
                               ( 
                               Q 
                               , 
                               S 
                               , 
                               C

                               μ 
                               ) 
                            
                          satisfying the witness condition as well as  (3) , which is fully informative and, whenever  
                            C 
                          satisfies  (4) , also irreducible. In general, for an arbitrary competence structure  
                            C 
                          on  
                            S 
                         , more than one (up to arbitrary choice of a domain bijective to  
                            Q 
                         ) fully informative and irreducible competence model can be constructed. Consider, for instance,

                            C

                               Q 
                               , 
                               S

                               C 
                               , 
                               μ 
                               ) 
                            
                          satisfying the witness condition and  (3)  is the only fully informative and irreducible competence model that can be defined on  
                            C 
                         , up to arbitrary choice of a domain bijective to  
                            Q 
                         . The proof follows from the fact that, whenever the atoms of  
                            C 
                          are singleton sets, the witness condition is necessary for a competence model to be fully informative ( Heller et al., 2017 ) and  (4)  is satisfied (because every  
                            C 
                          includes  
                            ∅ 
                         ).

Let  
                            
                               ( 
                               Q 
                               , 
                               S 
                               , 
                               C 
                               , 
                               μ 
                               ) 
                            
                          be a conjunctive competence model satisfying the witness condition. Then we can construct a domain restriction  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               S 
                               , 
                               C 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          of  
                            
                               ( 
                               Q 
                               , 
                               S 
                               , 
                               C 
                               , 
                               μ 
                               ) 
                            
                          satisfying the witness condition and  (3) , which turns out to be a fully informative competence model that does not contain redundant items. If  
                            C 
                          satisfies  (4) , then  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               S 
                               , 
                               C 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          is also irreducible (see  Proposition 1 ).

The procedures presented in this section allow for developing fully informative tests for a competence model that satisfies the witness condition or the generalized witness condition, where the competence structure  
                            C 
                          is given. We identified a certain class of competence models, for which the tests constructed from scratch or by shortening turn out to be irreducible. Notice that the witness and generalized witness conditions are not necessary for the tests to be fully informative, and can largely limit the number of tests that can be constructed in practical applications. Furthermore, there may be cases where it is difficult or impossible to find items the solution of which requires particular competencies. In Section  7 , a more general procedure is described that bypasses the structural constraints imposed on the underlying competence structure, and allows to confine consideration to particular items (e.g., those that are available or can be constructed) in the development of tests. For any arbitrary competence structure, the tests developed from scratch or by shortening turn out to be irreducible, and existing tests are augmented exclusively by items which are necessary to improve them.

Given a nonempty finite collection  
                         S 
                       of skills and a competence structure  
                         C 
                       defined on  
                         S 
                      , let  
                         
                            D 
                            ⊆ 
                            C

Definition 4 
                         Discernibility System 
                         A  discernibility system  is a quadruple

                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  D 
                                  , 
                                  T 
                                  , 
                                  δ 
                                  )

                               S

                               C 
                             is a nonempty collection of unordered pairs of competence states in the competence structure

                             defined on  
                               S

                                  T 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  
                                     
                                        2

                                  → 
                                  
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             is a mapping, called  discernibility function , that associates to each pair  
                               
                                  
                                     { 
                                     C 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           C 
                                        
                                        
                                           ′ 
                                        
                                     
                                     }

                                  D

                               T 
                             that discern the pair

                                  C

                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  }

                                     T

Proposition 2  connects some notions introduced above to results pertaining to conjunctive competence models provided by  Heller et al. (2017) .

                            { 
                            T 
                            } 
                         
                       is not in the range of  
                         δ 
                      . Competency deletion stops whenever  
                         
                            ( 
                            S 
                            , 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  ♢ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  δ 
                               
                               
                                  ♢ 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       is a reduct of  
                         
                            ( 
                            S 
                            , 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            T 
                            , 
                            δ 
                            ) 
                         
                       (see Section  7 ), and a reduct cannot be simplified any further.

As  Example 4  shows, there can be distinct reducts of a discernibility system  
                         
                            ( 
                            S 
                            , 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            T 
                            , 
                            δ 
                            ) 
                         
                       that result from deleting one competency in place of another, whenever there is a choice.

The  competency deletion procedure  is an iterative procedure that, given a discernibility system  
                         
                            ( 
                            S 
                            , 
                            D 
                            , 
                            T 
                            , 
                            δ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , constructs a reduct of it by deleting one competency from  
                         T 
                       at a time. The procedure works as illustrated in  Fig. 1 . With  
                         k 
                       being a scalar denoting the iteration of the procedure, let  
                         
                            ( 
                            S 
                            , 
                            D 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  δ 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       denote the discernibility system at iteration  
                         k 
                      . The initial discernibility system  
                         
                            ( 
                            S 
                            , 
                            D 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  δ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       at  
                         
                            k 
                            = 
                            0 
                         
                       is defined by  
                         
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            T 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               
                                  δ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            δ 
                         
                      . The procedure performs the following steps:

                                           T 
                                           }

                                        }

                                        T

                                  k

                            Stop at iteration  
                                  k 
                                if there is no competency that can be deleted, that is, if

                                to be deleted at  
                                  k

                                     k

                                that can be deleted at iteration  
                                  k 
                               .

Stop at iteration  
                                  k 
                                if there is no competency that can be deleted, that is, if  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           E 
                                        
                                        
                                           k

                                     ∅ 
                                  
                               .

If the stopping criterion is not satisfied at iteration  
                                  k 
                               , select from

                                the competency  
                                  E

It can be easily shown that the termination criterion is satisfied at iteration  
                         k 
                       if and only if the discernibility system  
                         
                            ( 
                            S 
                            , 
                            D 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  δ 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            )

                            S

                                  − 
                                  1

                                  − 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            )

                               E 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                       will not be empty and the termination criterion will not be met. From  Proposition 4  it follows that  
                         
                            ( 
                            S 
                            , 
                            D 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  δ

                            S

                            D 
                            ,

                                  0

The selection of the competency to be deleted at iteration  
                         k 
                       can proceed according to different rules. The simplest rule perhaps is to select one competency from  
                         
                            
                               E 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                       at random, which will be considered in the real-life examples presented in Section  8 . More sophisticated rules might be defined based on specific features of the particular situation, and the characteristics of the test one wants to obtain. Some viable options are suggested in Section  10 . Collecting all of the deletable competencies in  
                         
                            
                               E 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                       at each iteration may not seem necessary under random selection, where one may proceed with the first deletable competency found. Keeping the procedure more general, however, will be advantageous, if not necessary, whenever specific choice rules are to be applied to  
                         
                            
                               E 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                      .

The applications presented in this section refer to TIMSS (Trends in International Mathematics and Science Study;  https://timss.bc.edu/ ), a project of the International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement which continuously assesses the mathematics and science curricular achievement of fourth and eighth grade students in several countries. The applications come from work by  Su, Choi, Lee, Choi, and McAninch (2013)  on the TIMSS 2003 eighth grade mathematics test. Experts answered the items of this test and identified the skills that they used for solving each of them. In addition, based on mathematics grade levels arrangements of the American council called Council of Chief State School Officers ( National Governors Association Center for Best Practices and Council of Chief State School Officers, 2010 ), the experts identified a prerequisite relation among the skills. The associations between skills and items and the prerequisite relationships among the skills were validated on empirical data ( Su et al., 2013 ).

Three of the skills assessed by the TIMSS 2003 eighth grade mathematics test are:  
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                       Using ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, ( 
                         b 
                      ) Applying and extending previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions, and ( 
                         c 
                      ) Reasoning about and solving one-variable equations and inequalities. According to  Su et al. (2013) , skill  
                         b 
                       is a prerequisite of skill  
                         c 
                      . Thus, the competence structure defined on the collection  
                         
                            S 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                               , 
                               c 
                               } 
                            
                         
                       of the three skills turns out to be  
                         
                            (12) 
                            
                               
                                  C 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     ∅ 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     c 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     b 
                                     c 
                                     } 
                                  
                                  , 
                               
                            
                         
                      and is used throughout this section.

We want to construct a test that should allow for singling out a unique competence state in  
                            C 
                          from the knowledge state. Since the test should be able to discern any competence state in  
                            C 
                          from the others,  
                            D 
                          is set to be equal to the collection of all unordered pairs of competence states in  
                            C 
                         . Let  
                            T 
                          collect all competencies for which we have available, or can construct an item. Since skill  
                            b 
                          is a prerequisite of skill  
                            c 
                         , one might wonder if an item that requires  
                            c 
                          but not  
                            b 
                          can be constructed in practice. There are such items in the TIMSS 2003 eighth grade mathematics test. For instance, according to  Su et al. (2013) , item M012002 (“The objects on the scale make it balance exactly. One the left pan there is a 1 kg weight (mass) and half a brick. On the right pan there is one brick. What is the weight (mass) of one brick?”) requires skill  
                            c 
                          only. Although skill  
                            c 
                          cannot be learned without having available its prerequisite  
                            b 
                         , the latter plays no role in the problem’s solution path. In the following, the collection  
                            T 
                          is set to be equal to  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                               
                               ∖ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  ∅ 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                         , which amounts to assuming that we have an item for every nonempty subset of  
                            
                               S 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  , 
                                  c 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                         , that is,  
                            
                               T 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  , 
                                  c 
                                  , 
                                  a 
                                  b 
                                  , 
                                  a 
                                  c 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  c 
                                  , 
                                  a 
                                  b 
                                  c 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                         . It is worth noting that this assumption is not inevitable: in those cases where items requiring particular competencies to be solved are difficult to find or do not exist,  
                            T 
                          can be a proper subset of  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                               
                               ∖ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  ∅ 
                                  }

Rough set theory (RST;  Pawlak, 1982, 1991 ) derives from fundamental research on logical properties of  information systems  (or  information tables ), each of them being a quadruple

                            U 
                            , 
                            A 
                            , 
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     a

                                  a 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                                  } 
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        I 
                                     
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                                  | 
                               
                               a 
                               ∈ 
                               A 
                               } 
                            
                            ) 
                         
                      , where  
                         U 
                       is a nonempty finite set of objects,  
                         A 
                       is a nonempty finite set of attributes,  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               a 
                            
                         
                       is a nonempty set of values of attribute  
                         
                            a 
                            ∈ 
                            A 
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            
                               
                                  I

                            U

                                  V

                      , associates a value in

                       ( Pawlak, 1982; Yao & Zhao, 2009 ). One of the most important goals of RST is to reduce the complexity of an information system without losing the possibility of describing the objects properly. That is, with

                               A

                       as

                                     A 
                                     )
