                                     { 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        u 
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              u 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     ∈ 
                                     U 
                                     × 
                                     U 
                                     : 
                                     ∀ 
                                     a 
                                     ∈ 
                                     A 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           I 
                                        
                                        
                                           a

                                        u

                            R

                            A

                               R

                            IND

                               A

                      . Such a minimal subset of attributes is called a reduct.

There are strong connections between CbTD and RST. Roughly speaking, objects and attributes of RST correspond to competence states and competencies of CbTD, respectively. Moreover, the reduct of a discernibility system introduced in the present work corresponds to the reduct of an information system introduced in RST. The idea of constructing a reduct by deleting competencies has been inspired by  Yao & Zhao ’s ( 2009 ) work on information systems.

A major difference between RST and CbTD lies in the nature of the relation that is established between the elements under consideration. In RST, the relation between objects and attributes could be any and is derived empirically. In CbTD, the relation between competence states and competencies is that of set inclusion, and thus is established a priori. This allows for predicting the functioning of a hypothetical item associated with a certain competency even if this item does not exist and is to be developed yet. This is a fundamental aspect in the construction of a test from scratch and in the improvement of an existing test. In the applications of RST available in the literature, the attribute set is given and the goal is to reduce it without losing information. These applications correspond to the scenario where an existing test is shortened. To the best of our knowledge, there are no applications of RST where new attributes are added to the given attribute set. Thus, the scenarios of constructing a test from scratch and improving an existing test are innovative from an RST perspective.

In contrast to the information systems approach, discernibility systems allow to confine consideration to only a subset of pairs of competence states in  
                         C 
                      
                      
                         
                      
                      
                         C 
                       (i.e., the pairs in  
                         D 
                      ). This renders the construction of reducts more efficient and directly applicable in different scenarios. In the improvement of an existing test, for instance, considering only those pairs of competence states that must be distinguished instead of all pairs of competence states avoids the unwanted deletion of competencies that are associated with items already in the test and that need also to be included in the new test. The present work introduced competence-based test development (CbTD), a novel approach for the development of tests for skill assessment. Given the skills of interest in a certain disciplinary domain, it allows for constructing tests that are as informative as possible about individuals’ competence states. Applications of CbTD have been presented that cover the construction of a test from scratch, the improvement and shortening of an existing test. The methodology outlined in the previous sections concerns the development of conjunctive tests. The following elaborates on implications of the presented results, and outlines some paths for future research.

The duality between disjunctive and conjunctive models allows for constructing disjunctive tests using the same methodology described for conjunctive tests. Let us first consider a case which wants to make use of the canonical procedures. Given a competence structure  
                         C 
                       defined on a set  
                         S 
                       of skills, a disjunctive test for  
                         C 
                       can be constructed from scratch by applying the procedure described in Section  4  to the competence structure  
                         
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  ∂ 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               S 
                               ∖ 
                               C 
                               : 
                               C 
                               ∈ 
                               C 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      . Whenever a disjunctive test already exists and we want to improve or to shorten it, the corresponding procedures of Section  4  have to be applied to a conjunctive competence model  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  ∂ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                       obtained from the disjunctive competence model  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q

                            C

                       for all

                            Q 
                         
                      . In applications of the competency deletion procedure, we are given a competence structure  
                         C 
                       on  
                         S 
                      , and the collection

                       containing those pairs of competence states

                         T 
                       is the collection of all nonempty subsets  
                         
                            T 
                            ⊆ 
                            S 
                         
                       such that we can assume that an item requiring  any  skill  
                         
                            s 
                            ∈ 
                            T 
                         
                       for solving it can be constructed. In the case in which we want to improve or to shorten an already existing disjunctive test,  
                         T 
                       can be obtained by applying the same procedures as outlined in Sections  8.2 and 8.3  to  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  ∂ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                      . In its current form, the presented methodology does not allow for developing general tests, that is tests that allow for several solution strategies for the items without restricting them to require only one skill. An extension of the methodology in this direction is the subject of future work.

The proposed methodology can be simplified by introducing a collection of bags that contains neither empty bags nor bags that include nonempty bags. Removal of the empty bags is justified by the fact that they simply indicate that the competencies at hand do not allow for discerning some pairs of states. Removal of the bags including nonempty bags is justified by the fact that all the competencies which are in the superset bag but not in the subset bag are unnecessary. This operation corresponds to a set-version of the matrix absorption operation introduced by  Yao and Zhao (2009)  in the framework of RST.

At each iteration, the competency deletion procedure selects one competency to be deleted within a set of different competencies. The simplest rule is to select the competency to be deleted at random. However, more sophisticated rules can be implemented. For instance, the competency can be deleted that discerns the lowest number of pairs of competence states, or for which items associated with that competency are not available, or are difficult to construct. Other rules can be defined that mainly influence the inference of the knowledge state from observed responses to the items. For instance, the competency can be deleted that contains the largest number of skills. Alternatively, having available the estimates of careless error and lucky guess probabilities of the items (obtained, for instance, by applying the basic local independence model,  Falmagne & Doignon, 1988, 1988b ; or the competence-based local independence model,  Heller et al., 2015 ; to empirical data), one may delete the competency for which the items associated with it have large error and guessing probabilities. The last two rules are motivated by the fact that inference of the knowledge state from observed item responses is more accurate when test items require a few skills and do not have large error and guessing probabilities ( Heller & Repitsch, 2012; Liu, Huggins-Manley, & Bradshaw, 2017; Madison & Bradshaw, 2015 ). Future studies will aim at implementing these and other rules in the competency deletion procedure and to provide a detailed comparison of the performance of the resulting tests.

While the intention of some applications of RST seems to be in line with shortening an existing test, to the best of our knowledge there are no applications that correspond to constructing a test from scratch or improving an existing test. Future work might explore whether the presented results concerning these two scenarios can also be of interest to researchers in the area of RST.

The term “bag” is adapted from a draft by Jean-Paul Doignon (personal communication, August 4, 2018). We are grateful to the Acting Editor Jean-Paul Doignon for his valuable suggestions and comments. We also thank an anonymous reviewer for pointing out potential correspondences between conjunctive competence models and information systems.
